adapted_from: Zaharias, P. (2006). A usability evaluation method for e-learning: focus on motivation to learn.
administration: Administered once, after the post-test is submitted. All items are 5-point Likert. No right or wrong answers.

scale.interpretation: A mean of 3.00 or above on an item indicates a positive usability evaluation for that item; below 3.00 indicates a negative evaluation.

note: Item 7 and item 14 are negatively worded and must be reverse-scored (6 minus the response) before computing means. Reverse-scored items guard against acquiescence bias.

items.question_text: I could easily find my way around the system.
It was always clear to me where I was within a module.
Moving between lessons, scenarios, and quizzes was straightforward.
I could tell at any time how much of the course I had completed.
I understood how to use the system without needing anyone to explain it.
The purpose of each screen was clear to me.
I found the system unnecessarily complicated to use.
I would be able to use this system again after a break without relearning it.
The text was easy to read on the device I used.
The layout of the pages was clear and uncluttered.
The system worked well on my phone or the device I normally use.
Pages loaded quickly enough given my internet connection.
The feedback I received after quizzes helped me understand my mistakes.
I was often unsure whether my answers were right or wrong.
The consequences shown after each scenario choice made me think about my decision.
The system helped me see how my understanding changed as I progressed.
The content held my attention.
The scenarios felt relevant to situations I might actually face as a student.
Using this system made me want to learn more about the ethics of artificial intelligence.
I would recommend this system to another student.
